---
title: "Preface"
description: "Why this book exists, how to use it, and the notation and evidence conventions it follows."
page-role: frontmatter
---

A circuit rarely fails in the tidy way suggested by an exercise. A supply sags
under load, a probe changes the waveform, a component is outside its nominal
conditions, or software observes the right signal at the wrong time. Solving
such problems requires more than recognizing a circuit and selecting a formula.
It requires moving deliberately between the physical system, its models, and
the evidence.

That movement is the organizing idea of this book. The aim is to help you
explain what a circuit should do, observe what it actually does, account for any
difference, and decide what to change or what to accept. The treatment follows
the bench-first tradition of practical electronics [@horowitz2015art] and uses
the quantitative device and circuit models found in the standard
microelectronics literature [@sedra2020microelectronic].

## Who this book is for

This book serves self-directed learners, students, software engineers moving
closer to hardware, and practitioners who need a coherent reference outside
their usual specialty. You need no prior circuit theory at the start. The
appendices introduce algebra, calculus, probability, numerical methods,
datasheet reading, and reproducible engineering practice when those tools
become useful.

You do need a willingness to estimate before calculating, define a quantity
before measuring it, and revise a model when the observations disagree.

## What a chapter should give you

Each chapter explains a useful model, connects that model to observations, and
uses the comparison to support a design or diagnostic decision.

The chapters therefore combine explanation, worked calculations, executable
checks where they add value, practical procedures, and exercises. A simulation
is identified as simulation, synthetic teaching data as synthetic data, and a
physical measurement as measurement. These forms of evidence answer different
questions and are not interchangeable.

## Representations of the same system

Engineering work moves among several representations:

- the physical construction and its energy paths;
- the schematic, named nodes, and reference directions;
- equations with stated assumptions;
- simulation or another executable model;
- measured data and its uncertainty;
- software state and timing;
- requirements and the tests used to accept them.

An example need not include every representation. It should include the ones
needed to expose the relevant assumptions and support the decision being made.
Parameters remain visible in code and simulation, and raw observations should
remain available beside the analysis whenever practical.

## Notation and reporting conventions

SI units are used throughout [@bipm2019si]. A voltage polarity and current
reference direction are defined before an equation uses them. A negative value
then means the physical quantity opposes the chosen reference; it does not by
itself indicate an error.

The text distinguishes equality, approximation, tolerance, bounds, and
uncertainty. Signal amplitudes are identified as instantaneous, peak,
peak-to-peak, RMS, average, phasor, or spectral density as appropriate. Compact
models are accompanied by the operating conditions that make them useful.

## From prediction to a supported decision {#sec-evidence-workflow}

Evidence accumulates in stages. A quick estimate catches scale and sign errors;
a calculation tests a stated model; simulation makes a more detailed model
executable; measurement confronts the model with hardware. Repetition,
independent reproduction, and field experience then address variation and
generality. The final claim should be no stronger than the stages actually
completed.

![A common progression from prediction to a supported decision. Work may stop
at any stage when that evidence is sufficient for the consequence of being
wrong.](figures/evidence-workflow.svg){#fig-evidence-workflow
fig-alt="Estimate, calculation, and simulation form the prediction stage. Measurement and repetition form the observation stage. Independent reproduction and field evidence form the corroboration stage. The sequence ends in a supported decision."
width="88%" fig-pos="H"}

The progression in @fig-evidence-workflow is a guide to the weight of evidence, not a mandatory
checklist. A sign convention may need only a calculation. A component choice may
need calculation, simulation, and a bench test. Safety, compliance, reliability,
and lifecycle claims require evidence appropriate to their consequences and are
often governed by standards. Measurement uncertainty remains part of any
reported result [@jcgm2008gum].

The recurring working cycle is therefore **predict, build, measure, reconcile,
decide**. “Reconcile” means locating the source of a meaningful discrepancy:
the model, its parameters, the implementation, the instrument, or the
interpretation. It does not mean adjusting the explanation until the numbers
look agreeable.

## How to use the book

The sidebar gives the default route from foundations to systems. The
[reading roadmap](roadmap.qmd) provides shorter goal-oriented routes and explains
chapter maturity. Formal prerequisites are recorded in
[`chapters.toml`](chapters.toml). Appendix links provide support where a chapter
needs it without displacing the chapter from the main route.

For active study, pause before worked results and record an estimate. Reproduce
the calculation, identify the assumptions most likely to fail, and decide what
observation would distinguish competing explanations. When practical work is
appropriate, keep the source data, instrument settings, circuit revision, and
analysis together so another person can follow the result.

Course routes point to these same chapters rather than maintaining separate
copies of the theory. Instructors can use the
[instructor guide](../instructor/README.md) to turn sections into lessons and
practical work.

## Development and safety boundaries

This is a development edition. Chapter front matter reports maturity, and the
roadmap explains what each level supports. A draft may contain substantial
teaching material while still requiring technical and editorial review.

Early practical work uses current-limited, extra-low-voltage systems. Nothing in
this book authorizes work on mains, high voltage, high-energy batteries, RF
power, rotating machinery, medical equipment, or safety-critical systems. Such
work requires suitable competence, facilities, procedures, standards, and local
authorization.

## Sources

The reference spine includes practical electronics [@horowitz2015art],
microelectronic circuits [@sedra2020microelectronic], signals and systems
[@oppenheim1996signals], digital design and computer architecture
[@harris2021digital], feedback systems [@astrom2021feedback], electromagnetic
compatibility [@ott2009electromagnetic], the SI [@bipm2019si], and measurement
uncertainty [@jcgm2008gum]. Chapters add primary and canonical sources as their
technical content requires. The complete list is in the
[bibliography](references.qmd).

[Safe bench practice](01-foundations/f01-safe-practice.qmd) begins the default
route through the book.
